does: This paper investigates which entities associated with large language models should be identified as minds through mechanistic interpretability. Using empirical work on persona vectors and persona space, the authors identify and analyze three candidate views: the virtual instance view, which grounds minds in attention streams that sustain quasi-psychological connections across token-time; the virtual instance-persona view; and the model-persona view. They organize existing persona literature around three hypotheses about the internal structure underlying personas in LLMs, arguing that the persona-based views represent promising alternatives to traditional approaches for understanding LLM individuation and mental states.
connects: This work builds on recent mechanistic interpretability research investigating the internal structure of large language models, particularly empirical studies of persona vectors and persona space. It engages with philosophical literature on personal identity and individuation while applying these frameworks to AI systems. The paper responds to emerging concerns about LLM alignment and behavioral inconsistency through the lens of personas, drawing on prior work examining how LLMs maintain distinct behavioral patterns and voice characteristics. It connects to broader questions in philosophy of mind about consciousness and identity, while grounding discussion in concrete neural mechanisms rather than abstract theorizing about AI sentience or minds.
enables: This framework enables future research into how LLM behavior can be predicted and controlled by understanding the mechanistic basis of personas and identity. By articulating clear criteria for what constitutes a mind in LLMs, the work opens pathways for more rigorous investigation of LLM alignment, particularly regarding consistency and misalignment between different personas. It suggests that persona vectors could be targets for interpretability and safety research, potentially leading to methods for identifying, tracking, and managing multiple behavioral modes within single models. The theoretical framework may also inform approaches to multi-agent simulation and provide conceptual tools for studying emergent identity structures in future AI systems.
